==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}
==External Links== {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ {{ill|Yumoto Onsen Association|ja|湯元温泉組合}]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|湯の花" to "|湯の花|1=Yunohana"
revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{Japanese Hot Spring | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}

Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.

In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{Japanese Hot Spring | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}
==External Links== {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ {{ill|Yumoto Onsen Association|ja|湯元温泉組合}]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]]

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{Japanese Hot Spring | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{Japanese Hot Spring | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)

The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.

Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}
==External Links== {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ {{ill|Yumoto Onsen Association|ja|湯元温泉組合}]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]]

Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>

==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}
==External Links== {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ {{ill|Yumoto Onsen Association|ja|湯元温泉組合}]][[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]]
