revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市|12=draft}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅|12=draft}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光|12=draft}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|12=draft}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=draft}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|12=draft}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市|12=draft}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|12=draft}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|12=draft}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|12=draft}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|12=draft}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親|12=draft}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|12=draft}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|12=draft}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|12=draft}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|12=draft}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|12=draft}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|12=draft}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|12=draft}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑|12=draft}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|12=draft}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|12=draft}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|12=draft}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|12=draft}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|12=draft}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|12=draft}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|12=draft}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国|12=draft}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市|12=draft}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|12=draft}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|12=draft}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|12=draft}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|12=draft}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|12=draft}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|12=draft}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|12=draft}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|12=draft}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|12=draft}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード|12=draft}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|12=draft}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社|12=draft}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|12=draft}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|12=draft}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉|12=draft}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|12=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|12=draft}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|12=draft}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|12=draft}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu|12=draft}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|12=draft}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|12=draft}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|12=draft}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|12=draft}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|12=draft}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|12=draft}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|12=draft}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|12=draft}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|12=draft}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅|12=draft}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅|12=draft}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光|12=draft}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|12=draft}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|12=draft}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線|12=draft}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅|12=draft}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線|12=draft}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅|12=draft}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
==References== {{Footnote Help}} {{Reflist}}
==External Links== {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ {{ill|Yumoto Onsen Association|ja|湯元温泉組合}]]

revision.text:
{{日本の温泉地 |名称 = 日光湯元温泉 |画像 = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg |コメント = |所在地 = [[栃木県]][[日光市]] |交通アクセス = [[東武日光駅]]または[[日光駅]]より中禅寺温泉・赤沼・光徳温泉経由湯元温泉行きバス（[[東武バス日光]]）で約80 - 85分 |泉質 = [[硫黄泉]] |泉温 = |湧出量 = |pH = |液性の分類 = |浸透圧の分類 = |宿泊施設数 = 23 |総収容人員数 = |年間浴客数 = |統計年度 = |外部リンク = [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ 湯元温泉組合] |特記事項 = }} '''日光湯元温泉'''（にっこうゆもとおんせん）は、[[栃木県]][[日光市]]<ref>旧国名・[[下野国]]。</ref>[[奥日光]]の[[湯ノ湖]]畔、[[金精峠]]の麓にある[[温泉]]。
== 歴史 == [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[小林清親]]『日光湯元温泉』（1896年・明治29年）]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>湯ノ平湿原にある源泉</center>]] [[788年]]（延暦7年）、[[四本龍寺]]（紫雲立寺）を建立した[[勝道]]によって発見されたのが発端という。冬の寒さが厳しいため、昭和の初期までは主に夏だけの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]だった<ref>『日本地名大百科』、小学館、1996年、p.897 ISBN 4-09-523101-7</ref>。
[[江戸時代]][[文政]]年間に成立した「日光山志」（[[植田孟縉]]著）では日光湯元温泉を'''中禅寺温泉八湯'''（ちゅうぜんじおんせん はちゆ）と記しており<ref name = nikkosan-shi>[[植田孟縉]]著 「日光山志」</ref>、[[明治]]期に編纂され刊行された「[[古事類苑]]」ではこの書を引用し'''日光温泉'''（にっこうおんせん）としている<ref name = koji-ruien>[[明治政府]]編纂 「[[古事類苑]]」</ref>。
「日光山志」によると、中禅寺温泉湯元は[[中禅寺]]別所の西北、赤沼原（現在の赤沼）を経て[[里 (尺貫法)|三里]]、[[神橋|日光神橋]]より六里のところ、[[湯ノ湖]]の近くにあった。春に入っても風雪が激しく3月中は寒さが残り9月には前山が初雪となるため、毎年旧暦[[4月8日 (旧暦)|4月8日]]から同[[9月8日 (旧暦)|9月8日]]まで湯室を開いていたが、それでも[[日光白根山|白根山]]に残雪が残る期間は入浴する者も少なく、湯治シーズンは毎年5月末より6月頃からとなっていた。日光町方により管理され、必要な物資食料は日光町方が背に担いで運搬していた。三町から四町ほどの広さの平坦地・湯平（現在の湯元）には9軒の大きな[[湯屋]]があり、いずれも泉源のある東寄りの山際に並んで建てられており、ここから[[上野国|上州]][[沼田市|沼田]]に抜ける[[間道]]があった。 # 河原湯 ： 湯は非常に熱く、湖水の水位が高い時ほど熱く、低い時ほど温かい。 # 薬師湯 ： 眼病によい。 # 姥湯 ： 黒く苦味がある。 # 瀧湯 ： 非常に冷たい。 # 中湯 ： 熱い。 # 笹湯 ： 寒暑の湿疹（湿）をはらう。 # 御所湯 ： 第一金瘡に効く。 # 荒湯 ： 熱湯である。 # 自在湯 ： 混じり気が無い。洪水の時など遣い水が不自由である時など、この湯でご飯を炊いても匂いがしない。
また[[1878年]]（明治11年）6月、[[イザベラ・バード]]は日光滞在中に当地を訪れて八島屋に宿泊し、自身の本国への手紙に湯元温泉の活況ぶりを記した<ref>『イザベラ・バードの日本紀行』（[[講談社]]）</ref>。
それによると、湯元温泉は当時[[リウマチ]]や頑固な[[皮膚病]]に効能のある温泉として有名で、大勢の[[湯治]]客によりごった返していた。また宿屋は内外ともに清潔で畳は白く襖は軽く香気を放っており、宿泊客はお茶やお茶請けの菓子、凍った雪でもてなされていたという。
それ以外にバードは、湯元には密集した村落があったこと、村落の住宅は赤い[[ビャクシン属|柏槙]]材のきれいな住まいであったこと、住人は毎年[[10月10日]]に冬に備え住宅を[[むしろ]]で包み翌年[[5月10日]]まで週交代の1人の当番を残して低地で暮らしていたこと、湯元の入口には[[露天風呂]]があったこと、村の[[浴場]]は4箇所あったこと、村の背後には四角い浴槽の大きな温泉があり湯温は[[華氏]]130度（[[摂氏]]54.4度）であったこと、冬は3メートルの積雪があったことなどを書き残している。
[[1954年]]（昭和29年）、湯元温泉は[[酸ヶ湯|酸ヶ湯温泉]]および[[四万温泉]]とともに[[国民保養温泉地]]の第一号指定を受けた<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=国民保養温泉地について|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=日本温泉協会}}</ref>。
== 泉質 == * [[硫黄泉]]<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=1200年の歴史を誇る秘湯「日光湯元温泉」。時の流れを忘れて温泉三昧の旅へ|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=東武鉄道}}</ref> 源泉温度：49.3度～78.9度 ｐＨ＝6.33～6.6（弱酸性の硫化水素型硫黄泉） ちなみに中性はｐＨ＝7.0
== 温泉街 == [[ファイル:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|温泉街]] [[ファイル:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|あんよのゆ（[[足湯]]）]] 湯ノ湖畔に約23軒のホテル・旅館があり、同じ日光市内にある[[鬼怒川温泉]]のような歓楽色は全くない。湯には[[湯の花]]が浮かび、温泉街には温泉地ならではの硫黄臭が漂う。温泉街の中央には[[足湯]]施設「あんよのゆ」があり、無料で利用できる<ref name="tobu" />。{{要出典範囲|ところどころに廃業した旅館が残っている|date=2022年7月}}。
源泉地は温泉街のはずれの湯ノ平湿原にある。各源泉は屋根によって保護されている。ここの源泉は日光湯元の各旅館への配湯だけでなく、近くの[[光徳温泉]]や遠く離れた[[中禅寺温泉]]まで分湯されている。
源泉地の隣には[[輪王寺|日光山輪王寺]]別院の[[温泉寺 (日光市)|温泉寺]]がある。これは8世紀に勝道が当温泉を発見した際に建立し、その後途絶えていたもので、現存する構造物は[[1973年]]（昭和48年）に[[輪王寺]]によって建てられたものである。お寺にも温泉が引かれており、参拝客は男女別の[[共同浴場]]として利用できる<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=温泉寺|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=輪王寺}}</ref>。
== 交通 == * [[東武日光線]][[東武日光駅]]または[[日光線]][[日光駅]]より中禅寺温泉・赤沼・光徳温泉経由湯元温泉行きバス（[[東武バス日光]]）で約80 - 85分。 * [[上越新幹線]][[上毛高原駅]]または[[上越線]][[沼田駅]]より鎌田営業所方面行きバス（[[関越交通]]）で「鎌田」乗り換え、「日光・尾瀬かたしなエクスプレス号」（季節運行・運行日注意）、「湯元温泉線」（夏季限定運行）で約60分。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ 奥日光湯元温泉旅館協同組合オフィシャルサイト]
{{温泉}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:につこうゆもとおんせん}} [[Category:栃木県の温泉]] [[Category:国民保養温泉地]] [[Category:奥日光]] [[Category:日光市の地理]] [[Category:とちぎの百様]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[日光湯元温泉]] to [[Nikkō Yumoto Onsen]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{日本の温泉地 |名称 = 日光湯元温泉 |画像 = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg |コメント = |所在地 = [[栃木県]][[日光市]] |交通アクセス = [[東武日光駅]]または[[日光駅]]より中禅寺温泉・赤沼・光徳温泉経由湯元温泉行きバス（[[東武バス日光]]）で約80 - 85分 |泉質 = [[硫黄泉]] |泉温 = |湧出量 = |pH = |液性の分類 = |浸透圧の分類 = |宿泊施設数 = 23 |総収容人員数 = |年間浴客数 = |統計年度 = |外部リンク = [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ 湯元温泉組合] |特記事項 = }} '''日光湯元温泉'''（にっこうゆもとおんせん）は、[[栃木県]][[日光市]]<ref>旧国名・[[下野国]]。</ref>[[奥日光]]の[[湯ノ湖]]畔、[[金精峠]]の麓にある[[温泉]]。
== 歴史 == [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[小林清親]]『日光湯元温泉』（1896年・明治29年）]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>湯ノ平湿原にある源泉</center>]] [[788年]]（延暦7年）、[[四本龍寺]]（紫雲立寺）を建立した[[勝道]]によって発見されたのが発端という。冬の寒さが厳しいため、昭和の初期までは主に夏だけの[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]だった<ref>『日本地名大百科』、小学館、1996年、p.897 ISBN 4-09-523101-7</ref>。
[[江戸時代]][[文政]]年間に成立した「日光山志」（[[植田孟縉]]著）では日光湯元温泉を'''中禅寺温泉八湯'''（ちゅうぜんじおんせん はちゆ）と記しており<ref name = nikkosan-shi>[[植田孟縉]]著 「日光山志」</ref>、[[明治]]期に編纂され刊行された「[[古事類苑]]」ではこの書を引用し'''日光温泉'''（にっこうおんせん）としている<ref name = koji-ruien>[[明治政府]]編纂 「[[古事類苑]]」</ref>。
「日光山志」によると、中禅寺温泉湯元は[[中禅寺]]別所の西北、赤沼原（現在の赤沼）を経て[[里 (尺貫法)|三里]]、[[神橋|日光神橋]]より六里のところ、[[湯ノ湖]]の近くにあった。春に入っても風雪が激しく3月中は寒さが残り9月には前山が初雪となるため、毎年旧暦[[4月8日 (旧暦)|4月8日]]から同[[9月8日 (旧暦)|9月8日]]まで湯室を開いていたが、それでも[[日光白根山|白根山]]に残雪が残る期間は入浴する者も少なく、湯治シーズンは毎年5月末より6月頃からとなっていた。日光町方により管理され、必要な物資食料は日光町方が背に担いで運搬していた。三町から四町ほどの広さの平坦地・湯平（現在の湯元）には9軒の大きな[[湯屋]]があり、いずれも泉源のある東寄りの山際に並んで建てられており、ここから[[上野国|上州]][[沼田市|沼田]]に抜ける[[間道]]があった。 # 河原湯 ： 湯は非常に熱く、湖水の水位が高い時ほど熱く、低い時ほど温かい。 # 薬師湯 ： 眼病によい。 # 姥湯 ： 黒く苦味がある。 # 瀧湯 ： 非常に冷たい。 # 中湯 ： 熱い。 # 笹湯 ： 寒暑の湿疹（湿）をはらう。 # 御所湯 ： 第一金瘡に効く。 # 荒湯 ： 熱湯である。 # 自在湯 ： 混じり気が無い。洪水の時など遣い水が不自由である時など、この湯でご飯を炊いても匂いがしない。
また[[1878年]]（明治11年）6月、[[イザベラ・バード]]は日光滞在中に当地を訪れて八島屋に宿泊し、自身の本国への手紙に湯元温泉の活況ぶりを記した<ref>『イザベラ・バードの日本紀行』（[[講談社]]）</ref>。
それによると、湯元温泉は当時[[リウマチ]]や頑固な[[皮膚病]]に効能のある温泉として有名で、大勢の[[湯治]]客によりごった返していた。また宿屋は内外ともに清潔で畳は白く襖は軽く香気を放っており、宿泊客はお茶やお茶請けの菓子、凍った雪でもてなされていたという。
それ以外にバードは、湯元には密集した村落があったこと、村落の住宅は赤い[[ビャクシン属|柏槙]]材のきれいな住まいであったこと、住人は毎年[[10月10日]]に冬に備え住宅を[[むしろ]]で包み翌年[[5月10日]]まで週交代の1人の当番を残して低地で暮らしていたこと、湯元の入口には[[露天風呂]]があったこと、村の[[浴場]]は4箇所あったこと、村の背後には四角い浴槽の大きな温泉があり湯温は[[華氏]]130度（[[摂氏]]54.4度）であったこと、冬は3メートルの積雪があったことなどを書き残している。
[[1954年]]（昭和29年）、湯元温泉は[[酸ヶ湯|酸ヶ湯温泉]]および[[四万温泉]]とともに[[国民保養温泉地]]の第一号指定を受けた<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=国民保養温泉地について|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=日本温泉協会}}</ref>。
== 泉質 == * [[硫黄泉]]<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=1200年の歴史を誇る秘湯「日光湯元温泉」。時の流れを忘れて温泉三昧の旅へ|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=東武鉄道}}</ref> 源泉温度：49.3度～78.9度 ｐＨ＝6.33～6.6（弱酸性の硫化水素型硫黄泉） ちなみに中性はｐＨ＝7.0
== 温泉街 == [[ファイル:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|温泉街]] [[ファイル:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|あんよのゆ（[[足湯]]）]] 湯ノ湖畔に約23軒のホテル・旅館があり、同じ日光市内にある[[鬼怒川温泉]]のような歓楽色は全くない。湯には[[湯の花]]が浮かび、温泉街には温泉地ならではの硫黄臭が漂う。温泉街の中央には[[足湯]]施設「あんよのゆ」があり、無料で利用できる<ref name="tobu" />。{{要出典範囲|ところどころに廃業した旅館が残っている|date=2022年7月}}。
源泉地は温泉街のはずれの湯ノ平湿原にある。各源泉は屋根によって保護されている。ここの源泉は日光湯元の各旅館への配湯だけでなく、近くの[[光徳温泉]]や遠く離れた[[中禅寺温泉]]まで分湯されている。
源泉地の隣には[[輪王寺|日光山輪王寺]]別院の[[温泉寺 (日光市)|温泉寺]]がある。これは8世紀に勝道が当温泉を発見した際に建立し、その後途絶えていたもので、現存する構造物は[[1973年]]（昭和48年）に[[輪王寺]]によって建てられたものである。お寺にも温泉が引かれており、参拝客は男女別の[[共同浴場]]として利用できる<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=温泉寺|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=輪王寺}}</ref>。
== 交通 == * [[東武日光線]][[東武日光駅]]または[[日光線]][[日光駅]]より中禅寺温泉・赤沼・光徳温泉経由湯元温泉行きバス（[[東武バス日光]]）で約80 - 85分。 * [[上越新幹線]][[上毛高原駅]]または[[上越線]][[沼田駅]]より鎌田営業所方面行きバス（[[関越交通]]）で「鎌田」乗り換え、「日光・尾瀬かたしなエクスプレス号」（季節運行・運行日注意）、「湯元温泉線」（夏季限定運行）で約60分。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} * [http://www.nikkoyumoto.com/ 奥日光湯元温泉旅館協同組合オフィシャルサイト]
{{温泉}}

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Tochigi’s Diverse]]
revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō City|qq=}} | Access = From {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobu Nikko Station|qq=}} or {{ill|Nikkō Station|ja|日光駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Station|qq=}}, take a bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobu Bus Nikko|qq=}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in {{ill|Tochigi Prefecture|ja|栃木県|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tochigi Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Nikkō City|ja|日光市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō City|qq=}}<ref>Old province: {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja|下野国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimotsuke Province|qq=}}</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Kobayashi Kiyochika|ja|小林清親|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Kiyochika|qq=}}’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [{{ill|Meiji|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, in the {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “{{ill|Kojirui-en|ja|古事類苑|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojirui-en|qq=}}”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=}}) led to {{ill|Ueno Province|ja|上野国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueno Province|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Jōshū|ja|上州|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōshū|qq=}}) and {{ill|Numata City|ja|沼田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numata City|qq=}}.
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), {{ill|Isabella Bird|ja|イザベラ・バード|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isabella Bird|qq=}} visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher={{ill|Kodansha|ja|講談社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kodansha|qq=}}}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=}} and {{ill|Shimman Onsen|ja|四万温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimman Onsen|qq=}}, was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Tobu Railway|ja|東武鉄道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobu Railway|qq=}}}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ({{ill|foot bath|ja|足湯|1=Ashiyu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/foot bath|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiyu|qq=}})]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, {{ill|Kinugawa Onsen|ja|鬼怒川温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinugawa Onsen|qq=}}, which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either {{ill|Tobu Nikko Station|ja|東武日光駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobu Nikko Station|qq=}} or {{ill|Nikko Station|ja|日光駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikko Station|qq=}}, take the bus operated by {{ill|Tobu Bus Nikko|ja|東武バス日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tobu Bus Nikko|qq=}} via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the {{ill|Joetsu Shinkansen|ja|上越新幹線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joetsu Shinkansen|qq=}} at {{ill|Jōmō Kōgen Station|ja|上毛高原駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōmō Kōgen Station|qq=}} or from the {{ill|Joetsu Line|ja|上越線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joetsu Line|qq=}} at {{ill|Numata Station|ja|沼田駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Numata Station|qq=}}, take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}). According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. # {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.

In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

[[ja:日光湯元温泉]] [[zh:高湯温泉]] [[ja:草津温泉]] [[ja:万座温泉]] [[ja:野沢温泉]] [[ja:野湯]] [[zh:殺生石]] [[en:Sulfur springs]] [[ja:硫黄泉]] [[en:Nikkō Yumoto Onsen]] [[sn:Nikkō Yumoto Onsen]] [[Category:とちぎの百様]] [[Category:国民保養温泉地]]

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=}}).
