revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11504887]] Wikidata name: Nikkō Yumoto Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.

[[sn:Nikkō Yumoto Onsen]] [[Category:Hundred Aspects of Tochigi]] [[Category:National Recreation Hot Spring Resorts]] [[Category:Oku-Nikko]] [[Category:Geography of Nikko, Tochigi]] [[Category:Hot springs of Tochigi Prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Nikkō Yumoto Onsen}} [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11504887]] Wikidata name: Nikkō Yumoto Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Nikkō Yumoto Onsen (日光湯元温泉) | image = Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg | Comment = | Location = [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]] | Access = From [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikkō Station]], take a bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=|1=Chūzenji Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366836}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=|1=Akagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q118143810}} and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=|1=Kōtoku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388394}} to Yumoto Onsen (湯元温泉) in approximately 80–85 minutes. | Spring Quality = {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}} | Spring Temperature = | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 23 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Nikkō Yumoto Onsen''' (日光湯元温泉) is a hot spring located in [[:en:Tochigi Prefecture|Tochigi Prefecture]] [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō City]]<ref>Old province: [[:en:Shimotsuke Province|Shimotsuke Province]]</ref> on the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=|1=Lake Yunoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563442}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=|1=Okunikkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445815}}, at the foot of {{ill|Kinsei Pass|ja|金精峠|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinsei Pass|qq=|1=Konsei Pass|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11647790}}.
==History== [[File:NDL-DC 2542755-16 Kobayashi Kiyochika M291101 crd.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Kobayashi Kiyochika|Kobayashi Kiyochika]]’s "Nikkō Yumoto Onsen" (1896 [[[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 29)]]] [[File:Thermal springs, Yumoto, Nikko National Park, Tochigi, Japan.jpg|thumb|<center>Source in the Yunohira Wetland (湯ノ平湿原)</center>]] It is said that the origin of Nikkō Yumoto Onsen dates back to 788 (Enryaku 7), when it was discovered by {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=|1=Shōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11400506}}, who established {{ill|Shihonryū-ji|ja|四本龍寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shihonryū-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺|1=Rinnō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1348249}} (also known as {{ill|Shiunritsu-ji|ja|紫雲立寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiunritsu-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Because the winter cold was severe, until the early Shōwa period the site was used primarily as a {{ill|therapeutic bathing site|ja|湯治場|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/therapeutic bathing site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 湯治|1=thermal bath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1004887}}.
During the Edo period, in the [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] the work “Nikkō Sanshi” by {{ill|Ueda Mōkan|ja|植田孟縉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ueda Mōkan|qq=|1=Ueda Mōshin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11540684}} described Nikkō Yumoto Onsen as part of the “{{ill|Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|ja|中禅寺温泉八湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen Hachiyu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”, and in the Meiji period the work “[[:en:Koji Ruien|Kojirui-en]]”, compiled by the {{ill|Meiji Government|ja|明治政府|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Government|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治維新|1=Meiji restoration|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8707}}, quoted that work and referred to it simply as “Nikkō Onsen” (日光温泉).
According to “Nikkō Sanshi”, the Yumoto of Chūzenji Onsen was located to the northwest of the Chūzenji branch of the temple, and by way of the Akanumahara (now known as Akanum), then about three ri from there and six ri from {{ill|Nikkō Shinbashi|ja|日光神橋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shinbashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, it was near {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=|1=Lake Yunoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563442}}. Even as spring arrived, fierce winds and snow persisted through March, and by September the mountains received their first snowfall; therefore, every year the bathing rooms were open from the old calendar date of {{ill|April 8 (old calendar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (old calendar)|qq=|1=8th day of the 4th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839007}} to {{ill|September 8 (old calendar)|ja|9月8日 (旧暦)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 8 (old calendar)|qq=|1=8th day of the 9th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q840354}}, although during periods when {{ill|Nikkō Shirane|ja|白根山|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō Shirane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白根|1=Shirane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q16883214}} still retained snow few bathed. The site was managed by the Nikkō town authorities, who even carried necessary supplies and food on their backs. On a flat area known as Yuhira (currently Yumoto) covering roughly three to four chō, nine large bathhouses were built in a row along the eastern mountainside where the spring source was located; from here, a passage ({{ill|Machidō|ja|間道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 抜け道|1=rat running|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1322321}}) led to [[:en:Kōzuke Province|Ueno Province]] (also known as [[:en:Jōshū|Jōshū]]) and [[:en:Numata, Gunma|Numata City]].
# {{ill|Kawahara-yu|ja|河原湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawahara-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: The water is extremely hot; it becomes hotter when the lake water level is high and warmer when it is low. # {{ill|Yakushi-yu|ja|薬師湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Beneficial for eye diseases. # {{ill|Uba-yu|ja|姥湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uba-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Dark and bitter. # {{ill|Taki-yu|ja|瀧湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taki-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Extremely cold. # {{ill|Naka-yu|ja|中湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naka-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Hot. # {{ill|Sasa-yu|ja|笹湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasa-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Alleviates dampness-related skin conditions. # {{ill|Goshō-yu|ja|御所湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshō-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Effective against the first stage of kinsen. # {{ill|Ara-yu|ja|荒湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ara-yu|qq=|1=Ara-yu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q116479255}}: Boiling hot water. # {{ill|Jizai-yu|ja|自在湯|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizai-yu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pure and unadulterated; even when used for cooking rice during floods or water shortages, it does not impart any odor.
Also, in June 1878 (Meiji 11), [[:en:Isabella Bird|Isabella Bird]] visited Nikkō during her stay and lodged at {{ill|Yashima-ya|ja|八島屋|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yashima-ya|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, noting in her letter to her home country the bustling activity of Yumoto Onsen.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=イザベラ・バードの日本紀行|publisher=[[:en:Kodansha|Kodansha]]}}</ref>
According to her account, Yumoto Onsen was famous at the time as a hot spring effective for rheumatism and stubborn skin diseases, attracting a vast number of therapeutic bathers. Moreover, the inns were impeccably clean both inside and out—the tatami were white, the sliding doors light and exuding a gentle fragrance—and guests were treated to tea, confectioneries served with the tea, and even frozen snow.
Furthermore, Bird recorded that Yumoto Onsen was surrounded by a densely populated village, whose houses were elegant residences constructed of red {{ill|Bashōshin Genus|ja|柏槙|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bashōshin Genus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イブキ|1=Juniperus chinensis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q157697}} wood; that the residents, preparing for winter, wrapped their houses in {{ill|mushiro|ja|むしろ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mushiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 莚|1=straw mat|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11603096}} on October 10 each year and maintained a rotating on-duty resident in the lowlands until May 10 of the following year; that there was an open-air bath at the entrance to Yumoto; that the village had four public bathhouses; that behind the village there was a large, square hot spring bath with water at 130°F (54.4°C); and that in winter, snowfall reached as high as 3 meters.
In 1954 (Shōwa 29), Yumoto Onsen, along with {{ill|Sungayu Onsen|ja|酸ヶ湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sungayu Onsen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 酸ヶ湯|1=Sukayu Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7635737}} and [[:en:Shima Onsen|Shimman Onsen]], was designated as the first {{ill|National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|ja|国民保養温泉地|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Public Resort Hot Spring Area|qq=|1=National Hot Spring Resort Areas|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421080}}.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.spa.or.jp/kokumin_spa/about/|title=About National Public Resort Hot Spring Areas|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Japan Onsen Association|ja|日本温泉協会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Onsen Association|qq=|1=Japan Spa Association|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q60987269}}}}</ref>
==Spring Quality== * {{ill|Sulfur Hot Spring|ja|硫黄泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sulfur Hot Spring|qq=|1=Sulfur Spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587679}}<ref name="tobu">{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.tobu.co.jp/odekake/area/nikko-kinugawa/nikko-kinugawa012.html|title=A secret hot spring “Nikkō Yumoto Onsen” boasting 1200 years of history – a journey into hot spring indulgence that makes one forget the passage of time|accessdate=2022-07-13|website=[[:en:Tobu Railway|Tobu Railway]]}}</ref> Spring Mouth temperature: 49.3°C to 78.9°C, pH = 6.33 to 6.6 (a weakly acidic, hydrogen sulfide-type sulfur hot spring). (Neutral water has a pH of 7.0.)
==Onsen Town== [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Hot Spring Town]] [[File:200801 Nikko Yumoto Onsen Nikko Tochigi pref Japan02s3.jpg|thumb|Anyo no Yu ([[:en:Ashiyu|Ashiyu]])]] There are about 23 hotels and ryokan along the shore of {{ill|Yunoko|ja|湯ノ湖|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunoko|qq=|1=Lake Yunoko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563442}} in {{ill|Oku-Nikkō|ja|奥日光|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oku-Nikkō|qq=|1=Okunikkō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11445815}}, and the onsen town completely lacks the hedonistic atmosphere of, for example, [[:en:Kinugawa Onsen|Kinugawa Onsen]], which is also located within Nikkō City. The hot spring water exhibits {{ill|Yunohana|ja|湯の花|1=Yunohana|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|13=User:Immanuelle/Yunohana|qq=}}, and the town is permeated with the characteristic sulfur scent of hot spring areas. At the center of the onsen town is a free foot bath facility called “Anyo no Yu”. {{Citation needed|Some abandoned ryokan remain in the town.|date=July 2022}}
The source of the hot spring is located on the outskirts of the onsen town, in the Yunohira Wetland. Each source is sheltered by a roof. The source water is distributed not only to the ryokan of Yumoto Onsen but also to nearby {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=|1=Kōtoku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388394}} and even to distant {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=|1=Chūzenji Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366836}}.
Adjacent to the source area is the Onsen-dera of the {{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺|1=Rinnō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1348249}} branch, known as Onsen-dera. Originally established in the 8th century when {{ill|Katsumichi|ja|勝道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumichi|qq=|1=Shōdō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11400506}} discovered the hot spring, the extant structure was rebuilt by Nikkō San Rinnō-ji in 1973 (Shōwa 48). The temple also has its own hot spring; visitors can use its gender-segregated communal bath.<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://www.rinnoji.or.jp/temple/onsenji/|title=Onsen-dera|accessdate=2022-07-13|website={{ill|Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|ja|日光山輪王寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nikkō San Rinnō-ji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 輪王寺|1=Rinnō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1348249}}}}</ref>
==Access== * Rail: From either [[:en:Tōbu–Nikkō Station|Tobu Nikko Station]] or [[:en:Nikkō Station|Nikko Station]], take the bus operated by [[:en:Tobu Bus Nikko|Tobu Bus Nikko]] via {{ill|Chūzenji Onsen|ja|中禅寺温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzenji Onsen|qq=|1=Chūzenji Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11366836}}, {{ill|Akagawa|ja|赤沼|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akagawa|qq=|1=Akagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q118143810}}, and {{ill|Kōtoku Onsen|ja|光徳温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōtoku Onsen|qq=|1=Kōtoku Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11388394}} to Yumoto Onsen for about 80–85 minutes. * Shinkansen: From the [[:en:Jōetsu Shinkansen|Joetsu Shinkansen]] at [[:en:Jōmō-Kōgen Station|Jōmō Kōgen Station]] or from the [[:en:Jōetsu Line|Joetsu Line]] at [[:en:Numata Station|Numata Station]], take a bus toward the Kamata Eigyōsho, then transfer to the “Nikkō-Oze Katashina Express” (seasonal; check schedule) or the “Yumoto Onsen Line” (summer limited operation) for about 60 minutes.
